upperAir: Sounding data unavailable for KOAK today. Based on model-derived boundary layer heights, the mixing layer is shallow in the morning (135–260m at Ed Levin by 10 AM) and peaks around 420–465m by early afternoon — suggesting a modest thermal ceiling well below 2000 ft AGL. No CAPE is present at any site or time, confirming stable, non-convective conditions. Wind shear is notable early: 80m winds at Ed Levin run 15–21 kts from the NE/NNE at 8–9 AM while surface winds are only 9–12 kts, indicating a significant shear layer that gradually collapses by midday.
aviationFlags: No active AIRMETs for the SF Bay Area. METAR anomalies: KHAF reporting sustained NNW winds 19–22 kts with gusts to 27–30 kts through the overnight and early morning hours, with 5–7SM visibility in haze — this is an active gap flow event funneling strong northerly flow through the coastal gap. KSFO reporting W 20 kts gusting to 29–30 kts overnight, moderating to 20 kts by 0056Z. These are pre-dawn observations but signal residual strong gradient flow that will influence morning conditions at all sites.

sites.name: Ed Levin (1750 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — south-facing ridge launch, thermal and ridge combo site
sites.howItWorks: Ed Levin's 1750 ft launch works best on W-NW sea breeze or light SW thermic flow in the afternoon. Morning launches can work with valley wind cycles, but the launch requires at least 8-12 kts on-face to soar; thermals develop mid-morning and can carry pilots well above launch when BL height is adequate.
sites.todaySetup: The deciding factor today is a messy morning wind regime followed by a transition to very light and variable conditions — neither phase is ideal for the high launch. Early morning brings NNE surface flow at 9–12 kts with a dangerous shear layer: 80m winds are 15–21 kts NNE while surface is only 9–12 kts, creating significant mechanical turbulence aloft during the critical climb-out phase. By 10 AM, cloud cover spikes to 95% (likely a marine layer push), which will suppress thermal development just as the shear begins to relax. After 11 AM the wind shifts to near-calm (4–8 kts from NW/WNW at 312–345°) and the BL height stabilizes around 405–430m — marginally soarable but thermals will be weak with zero CAPE and a shallow mixing layer well below launch altitude. The afternoon sea breeze at 6–9 kts from the WNW is the best window but remains light and thermally unproductive.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 8–12 kts surface with 15–21 kt winds at 80m — strong shear makes launch dangerous. Cloud cover builds to 95–100% by 10 AM, suppressing any thermal development. Not recommended.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts to light N/NW at 4–6 kts, shear collapses, skies clear. BL height 390–465m. Thermals possible but weak; soaring margin is thin at this altitude.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW sea breeze fills in at 5–7 kts, BL 395–425m. Conditions are light and stable — scratchy soaring at best, and cloud cover returns to 80–88% by 5 PM signaling marine layer return.

verdict.hazards: Strong morning shear (21 kts at 80m vs 12 kts surface), marine layer suppressing thermals, light winds barely adequate for launch, BL ceiling shallow (~430m)

sites.name: Ed Levin (600 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — mid-hill launch, W-facing, thermal and training site
sites.howItWorks: The 600 ft launch is more sheltered than the high launch and works well with moderate WNW sea breeze or valley thermal cycles. It's a forgiving site in smooth air but requires consistent on-face flow to soar; light or cross winds result in a sled ride.
sites.todaySetup: Similar story to the high launch but with an earlier opportunity as the shear layer is less extreme at this elevation. Morning NNE flow at 9–13 kts with 80m shear at 13–23 kts is still concerning but diminishes faster at this level. The real window opens around 12–1 PM when winds shift to light NW/WNW at 3–7 kts and skies clear — BL heights reach 420–465m, which is workable for this launch. Thermals will be weak with zero CAPE, but the valley floor heating in the afternoon can produce light punchy thermals. The afternoon WNW sea breeze at 7–9 kts (308–318°) is the best scenario for this launch, though the marine layer return by 5 PM signals the close of the day.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 9–13 kts surface, 80m winds 13–23 kts — shear is significant. Cloud cover 88% by 10–11 AM suppresses thermals. Sled ride or no-fly conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind calms to 3–7 kts NW/WNW, skies clear, BL 420–465m. Light thermals possible; best thermal window of the day. Flyable for patient P2+ pilots in smooth air.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW sea breeze 7–9 kts (308–318°) — on-face for this launch, consistent enough for soaring. BL holds at 435–440m. Best sustained soaring window.

verdict.hazards: Morning shear hazard, marine layer cloud suppressing early thermals, light afternoon winds may be borderline for sustained soaring, punchy thermals possible in valley heating phase

sites.name: Ed Levin (300 ft)
sites.locationDescriptor: Milpitas — low training hill, W-facing, student and H/G training site
sites.howItWorks: The 300 ft bunny hill is a training and sledder site. It works best with 8–15 kts on-face westerly flow for consistent ridge lift; thermals are occasional and light. It's appropriate for student flights and low airtime pilots in smooth conditions.
sites.todaySetup: The 300 ft launch sees the most benign version of today's conditions. Morning starts with very light surface flow (2–4 kts WNW) — too light for ridge soaring, resulting in short sled rides. By midday the WNW component fills in at 5–8 kts with a clear sky window and BL heights of 390–455m, which is adequate for training flights. The afternoon brings the most consistent flow at 7–9 kts from 293–307° — directly on-face — making this the best training window. No significant shear hazard at 300 ft and thermals are weak enough to be non-threatening for students under instructor supervision.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Very light surface winds 2–4 kts WNW, cloud cover building to 78–82%. Too light for sustained flight; sled rides only under instructor supervision.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind picks up to 5–8 kts WNW as skies clear. Light thermals possible. Good training window with smooth, manageable conditions.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Best window: 7–9 kts from 293–307° (WNW), directly on-face. BL 420–470m. Consistent soaring possible for this level; light thermals keep it interesting without being punchy.

verdict.hazards: Morning too light for sustained flight, marine layer clouds 8–11 AM, mild afternoon thermals within student tolerance under supervision

sites.name: Mussel Rock
sites.locationDescriptor: Daly City — coastal bluff, W-facing ridge soaring site
sites.howItWorks: Mussel Rock is a pure ridge lift site driven by onshore W-WSW sea breeze. Ideal window is 12–20 kts from 270–300°. Too light and it's a sled; too strong (>20 kts) and it becomes a rotor/turbulence hazard. Marine layer timing is critical — if the layer stays capped below the bluff top, soaring is excellent; if it moves on-shore and drops below the ridge, it shuts the site down.
sites.todaySetup: Mussel Rock is the most consistently flyable site today, but the KHAF METARs are a major red flag for the morning: overnight and pre-dawn winds were sustained 19–22 kts NNW with gusts to 27–30 kts, indicating strong gap flow that may still be lingering at dawn. This is a rotor/turbulence hazard on the approach and in the landing zone. By mid-morning, models show wind moderating to 4–6 kts from W/WSW at the surface — actually too light for consistent ridge soaring. The afternoon fills in with 8–10 kts from 262–282° (W to WNW), which is the sweet spot for this site. Cloud cover is the other concern: 100% overcast at 10 AM and 12 PM, with 87–90% at 11 AM and 4–5 PM. The afternoon clearing window (1–3 PM, 33–36% cloud cover) aligns with the best wind, making that the prime window.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Residual gap flow hazard from KHAF overnight gusts (27–30 kts NNW). Surface models show 4–6 kts by 9–11 AM but with turbulent boundary layer. 87–100% cloud cover. No fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind builds from 6–8 kts W/WSW as clouds partially clear (36–100% variable). 100% overcast at noon is concerning; wait for the 1 PM clearing. Best conditions developing by 1:30–2 PM.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: 9–10 kts from 262–282° — classic Mussel Rock sea breeze. 28–33% cloud cover allows good light. BL 255–320m. Best soaring window; watch for gusts and marine layer return by 5 PM (90–99% cloud).

verdict.hazards: Residual overnight gap flow turbulence in morning (KHAF gusts 27–30 kts pre-dawn), heavy cloud cover morning through noon, marine layer return by 5 PM, rotor potential in LZ from northerly remnant flow

sites.name: Mission Peak
sites.locationDescriptor: Fremont — inland ridge/thermal combo, S-facing, NW sea breeze site
sites.howItWorks: Mission Peak works on thermal cycles from valley heating plus afternoon NW sea breeze that provides consistent lift on the south-facing bowl. Ideal conditions: 8–15 kts NW/WNW with good sun and a BL height above 1500 ft for XC. Morning NE/N flow is cross or tailwind — not suitable for most launch aspects.
sites.todaySetup: Mission Peak faces the same morning shear problem as the Ed Levin high launch, but it's arguably worse here: 80m winds at 18–21 kts NNE while surface is 9–12 kts NNE at 8–9 AM creates dangerous shear for pilots trying to climb out. The NNE surface flow is also unfavorable for most launch aspects at Mission Peak. Cloud cover peaks at 95–100% by 10–11 AM, completely shutting down thermal development during the morning transition. The afternoon does improve — wind shifts to 312–355° at 4–7 kts surface with a BL of 395–430m — but thermals will be very weak (zero CAPE, shallow BL well below the peak elevation), and the wind is borderline light for consistent soaring. The upper-level winds (80m: 6–9 kts, 120m: 6–9 kts) don't indicate strong soaring potential. A scratchy afternoon is the best-case scenario.

hourlyWindows.label: Morning (8–11 AM)
hourlyWindows.summary: NNE 9–12 kts surface with 15–21 kt shear at 80m — dangerous for launch and climb-out. 95–100% cloud cover kills thermals. Hard no-fly.

hourlyWindows.label: Midday (11 AM–2 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: Wind shifts to light N/NNW at 4–6 kts as skies partially clear. BL 405–430m. Thermals weak; conditions marginal. Some scratchy soaring possible for patient pilots.

hourlyWindows.label: Afternoon (2–5 PM)
hourlyWindows.summary: WNW 5–7 kts (291–345°) with BL holding at 310–425m. Light sea breeze assists but thermals remain weak. Flyable but expect short soaring cycles; 88% cloud returns by 5 PM.

verdict.hazards: Severe morning wind shear (21 kts at 80m vs 12 kts surface), unfavorable NNE morning flow for launch aspects, marine layer cloud suppressing thermals until midday, BL ceiling too shallow for meaningful XC

tomorrow.name: Ed Levin (all launches)
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday March 22 looks noticeably better. Morning starts calm (BL only 35–55m at 8 AM) but quickly improves to light WNW 3–7 kts by midday with BL heights reaching 410–595m by afternoon. WNW 7–9 kts by 3–4 PM with BL at 595–680m — substantially deeper than today and no morning shear event. Best soaring day of the stretch for Ed Levin launches.

tomorrow.name: Mussel Rock
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday shows a clean W/WSW 5–7 kts at 10 AM building to 6–7 kts by afternoon from 272–283°. Cloud cover minimal (0–13%). No sign of the overnight gap flow that complicates today. Looks like a solid soaring day with consistent on-face flow and clear skies.

tomorrow.name: Mission Peak
tomorrow.analysis: Sunday improves significantly. BL rises to 580–640m by afternoon with WNW flow at 3–4 kts surface but 7–10 kts at 80m — indicating a stronger upper-level sea breeze. Light thermals possible with a deeper BL. Better than today but still modest.

day2.name: Ed Levin (all launches)
day2.analysis: Monday March 23 shows the deepest BL of the forecast window — up to 760m by 2 PM with WNW 7–8 kts (307–317°). Zero CAPE but the deeper mixing and consistent sea breeze makes this potentially the best thermal soaring day. Cloud cover minimal except late afternoon buildup (64% by 4 PM). Watch for marine layer return.

day2.name: Mussel Rock
day2.analysis: Monday brings W/WNW 6–8 kts building to 8 kts by 2 PM from 282–297°. BL 305–375m. Partly cloudy (20–33%) in the afternoon. Solid ridge soaring day — likely the best of the 3-day window for Mussel Rock.

day2.name: Mission Peak
day2.analysis: Monday looks good with BL reaching 725m by 2 PM. Surface winds light at 3–5 kts WNW but 80m winds at 9–10 kts indicate sea breeze aloft. XC potential for the first time this weekend.

day3.name: All sites
day3.analysis: No data available beyond Monday March 23 in this dataset. Based on the trend of a building sea breeze pattern with deepening BL each day, conditions are likely to remain soarable into the mid-week period. Monitor for any change in offshore gradient that could reinstate the strong NNW gap flow seen in the KHAF METARs overnight.

watchlist: KHAF overnight gusts 27–30 kts NNW — residual turbulence and rotor in coastal sites' LZs until mid-morning; re-check KHAF METAR before driving to Mussel Rock
Marine layer cloud cover at 95–100% forecast for Ed Levin and Mission Peak at 10–11 AM — this could linger and push the flyable window later than models suggest
Wind shear at Ed Levin and Mission Peak: 80m winds 15–21 kts NNE vs 9–12 kts surface through 9–10 AM — dangerous for high-launch climb-outs regardless of surface conditions
BL heights today are shallow (405–465m max) — all thermal sites have limited ceiling and XC is not recommended; stay local and near LZ options
Late afternoon marine layer return (80–88% cloud cover by 5 PM) across all sites — plan to be on final glide or landed by 4:30 PM
tldr: Today is a MARGINAL day across the board — morning gap flow residuals and a marine layer cloud surge at 10 AM make early flights a no-go at all sites. Your best bet is Mussel Rock from roughly 1:30–4 PM with 9–10 kts W/WSW once the clouds break, or Ed Levin 600 ft/300 ft for light afternoon thermic soaring from 1–4 PM. Sunday and Monday look progressively better with deeper BL and cleaner sea breeze — worth the wait.
